scaled_oracle_cases.description: Replay the canonical main() seed flow for compute_probs with original input order; output values are already sorted.
scaled_oracle_cases.semantic_intent: Verify that the output fragment lengths are sorted in ascending order even when input order is coincidentally sorted.
scaled_oracle_cases.target_constraint: The values list must be sorted ascending.
scaled_oracle_cases.expected_failure_mode: A solution that does not sort may still pass if input yields sorted results coincidentally.
scaled_oracle_cases.call_code:
values, probs = compute_probs(datalist)
print("Selected fragment lengths:", values)
print("Their probabilities:", probs)
result = {'values': values, 'probs': probs}
covered_requirements: Scaled executable cases must check that the first returned list is sorted in ascending order.

scaled_oracle_cases.description: Verify that output fragment lengths are sorted in ascending order when input data is provided in non-sorted order, ensuring the function explicitly sorts.
scaled_oracle_cases.semantic_intent: Verify that the output fragment lengths are sorted in ascending order regardless of input order.
scaled_oracle_cases.target_constraint: The values list must be sorted ascending even when input frequencies are given in a different order.
scaled_oracle_cases.expected_failure_mode: A solution that relies on the natural iteration order of set(datalist) would return values in non-sorted order (e.g., [12, 8, 10]) and fail the assertion or output check.
scaled_oracle_cases.call_code:
values, probs = compute_probs(datalist)
print("Selected fragment lengths:", values)
print("Their probabilities:", probs)
result = {'values': values, 'probs': probs}
covered_requirements: Scaled executable cases must check that the first returned list is sorted in ascending order.
